title: Probability Calculator — Basic, Permutations, Combinations, Dice
shortDescription: Calculate probabilities, permutations (nPr), combinations (nCr), and dice roll outcomes. Four dedicated tabs cover every common probability problem.
description: Four modes in one tool: basic probability from favorable and total outcomes, permutations nPr, combinations nCr, and the probability of rolling a specific sum with multiple dice. Results include fractions, decimals, percentages, and odds.
metaDescription: Free probability calculator: basic probability, permutations nPr, combinations nCr, and dice probability. Results as fraction, decimal, percentage, and odds.

error.invalid: Invalid input: check that all values are non-negative integers and r ≤ n.
error.noOutcomes: Total outcomes must be greater than zero.
error.favorableExceedsTotal: Favorable outcomes cannot exceed total outcomes.
error.diceRange: Target sum is outside the possible range for these dice.

sections.heading: Basic probability, odds, and how they differ
paragraphs: Probability is the ratio of favorable outcomes to total equally likely outcomes: P = favorable / total. It ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain) and is often expressed as a percentage. For a fair six-sided die, the probability of rolling a 4 is 1/6 ≈ 16.667%.

Odds are a different way to express the same information. Odds FOR an event are favorable : unfavorable, so rolling a 4 has odds of 1:5. Odds AGAINST are the reverse: 5:1. Odds are common in sports betting and card games; probability is more common in statistics and science.
This calculator simplifies the fraction to lowest terms automatically and shows decimal and percentage forms side by side, making it easy to convert between representations.

sections.heading: Permutations vs. combinations
paragraphs: Both deal with selecting r items from a set of n, but order matters in permutations and does not in combinations. Permutations nPr = n! / (n − r)! counts ordered arrangements. Combinations nCr = n! / (r! × (n − r)!) counts unordered subsets.

Example: choosing 2 letters from {A, B, C}. Permutations: AB, BA, AC, CA, BC, CB — 6 ordered pairs. nPr = 3! / 1! = 6. Combinations: AB, AC, BC — 3 unordered subsets. nCr = 3! / (2! × 1!) = 3. Because every combination gives r! permutations, nPr = nCr × r! always.
The maximum supported n is 170. Beyond that, n! exceeds JavaScript’s floating-point range (about 1.8 × 10³°⁸). For very large n or r, consider logarithmic calculation or symbolic libraries.

sections.heading: Dice probability and the multiplication rule
paragraphs: When rolling multiple identical dice, each die is independent. The total number of outcomes is sides^numDice. The number of ways to reach a specific sum is found by counting compositions: distributing the sum across all dice such that each die shows a value between 1 and sides.

This calculator uses dynamic programming to count the exact number of compositions for the given target sum. For example, rolling 2d6 (two six-sided dice) and getting a sum of 7: there are 6 ways (1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2, 6+1) out of 36 total, giving probability 6/36 = 1/6 ≈ 16.667%.

Supported dice types: d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, and d20 (standard tabletop RPG dice). The number of dice ranges from 1 to 6. The target sum must be between numDice (all ones) and numDice × sides (all maximums).

howTo.name: Choose a tab
howTo.text: Select Basic Probability, Permutations, Combinations, or Dice Probability depending on your problem.

howTo.name: Enter the inputs
howTo.text: For basic probability, provide favorable and total outcomes. For nPr/nCr, enter n and r. For dice, pick number of dice, target sum, and sides.

howTo.name: Read the results
howTo.text: Results appear instantly as a fraction, decimal, percentage, and odds (for basic probability).

howTo.name: Check for errors
howTo.text: If inputs are invalid (r > n, total = 0, or sum out of range), a clear error message explains what to fix.

faq.q: What is the difference between permutations and combinations?
faq.a: Permutations count ordered arrangements (AB ≠ BA); combinations count unordered subsets (AB = BA). nPr = n! / (n−r)!; nCr = n! / (r! × (n−r)!). Always nPr ≥ nCr for r > 1.
JavaScript uses 64-bit IEEE 754 floating-point. 170! ≈ 7.26 × 10³°⁶ fits in a double; 171! overflows to Infinity. If you need larger factorials, use BigInt or a logarithm-based approach.

faq.q: How are odds different from probability?
faq.a: Probability P = favorable / total. Odds For = favorable : unfavorable = P : (1−P). Odds Against = unfavorable : favorable. A 25% probability corresponds to odds of 1:3 (for) or 3:1 (against).
By convention, 0! = 1. This keeps the formulas for nCr and nPr consistent when r = 0 or r = n.

faq.q: Can I calculate the probability of rolling at least a certain sum?
faq.a: Not directly with this tool. For at-least probabilities, sum the exact probabilities for all target values from your minimum to the maximum (numDice × sides). The dice tab gives the exact probability for each specific sum.
Both equal 1. There is exactly one way to choose nothing (the empty set) and exactly one way to choose all items.

faq.q: Is this calculator accurate for large factorials?
faq.a: For n up to 170 the results are accurate within the floating-point representation. For n near 170, results may have small relative errors in the last few digits due to floating-point rounding in division.

faq.q: Does any data leave my browser?
faq.a: No. All calculations run locally in JavaScript. Nothing is transmitted to a server.
